coredata.dc:title: Facile, rapid synthesis of N,P-dual-doped carbon dots as a label-free multifunctional nanosensor for Mn(VII) detection, temperature sensing and cellular imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Du, Fangfang
Li, Gan
Gong, Xiaojuan
Zhonghui, Guo
Shuang, Shaomin
Xian, Ming
Dong, Chuan
coredata.dc:description: We rapidly synthesized N,P-dual-doped carbon dots (N,P-CDs) via the acid-base neutralization spontaneous heat using a simple blend of phthalic acid, 1,2-ethylenediamine, and concentrated phosphoric acid. The approach is automatic without external heat treatment to speedily produce dozens of N,P-CDs, which refrain high temperature, sophisticated manipulations, and long reaction times. As-fabricated N,P-CDs with excellent luminescent properties, superior biocompatibility and respectable fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of 15% have been employed for the development of a label-free multifunctional fluorescence sensing platform for the detection of Mn(VII), temperature sensing and cellular imaging. Mn(VII) is able to effectively quench the fluorescence of N,P-CDs based on inner filter effect (IFE) because the absorption bands of Mn(VII) can perfectly overlap the emission and excitation wavelengths of N,P-CDs. The system had high selectivity toward Mn(VII) and the detection limit was 48.3 nM in the broad linear span from 10 μM to 200μM. Moreover, the N,P-CDs were extended for sensing Mn(VII) in living cell due to favorable biocompatibility and negligible cytotoxicity. Furthermore, the sensors showed a linear and reversible variation of emission intensity for a temperature range from 25℃ to 80℃, with a sensitivity of −1.79% per ℃. The practical implications of N,P-CDs for Mn(VII) detection have been demonstrated in Teas leaves and Medicinal herbs samples with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N,P-dual-doped carbon dots
Manganese(VII) ion
Temperature sensing
Teas leaves and medicinal herbs samples
Cellular imaging

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optimal conditions for fluorescent detection of Mn(VII)
Specificity of the fluorescent probe for Mn (VII)
Principle of Mn(VII) assay based on IFE
Application of Mn(VII) detection in real tea samples and medicinal herb samples
In vitro cellular imaging determination of Mn(VII)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image, (b) size distribution of the as-synthesised N,P-CDs.
(a) XPS survey scan of N,P-CDs, (b) C1 s XPS, (c) N1 s XPS and (d) P2p XPS.

(a) The optical properties of N,P-CDs aqueous solutions. UV–vis absorption spectra (inset: photographs taken under visible light). Optimal excitation and emission FL spectra (inset: photographs taken under 365 nm UV ligtht). (b) FL spectra of N,P-CDs (1 mg mL−1).with different λex.280–460 nm.

(a) The FL spectra of the N,P-CDs in the temperature range 25–80℃(inset: the emission intensity as a function of temperature, respectively); (b) The reversible fluorescence response of eight consecutive cycles between 25 and 80℃, respectively.

(a) Fluorescence titration spectra of N,P-CDs in the presence of Mn(VII) (0–500 μM). Inset: The fluorescence intensities of N,P-CDs (1 mg mL−1) as a function of Mn(VII) concentration. (b) Temperature dependent FL of the Mn(VII)/ N,P-CDs system. (c) Response of fluorescence intensity of N,P-CDs (1 mg mL−1) after the addition of Mn(VII) (0.5 mM) or other different metal ions (0.5 mM) or anion ions (0.5 mM). (d) Response of fluorescence intensity of N,P-CDs (1 mg mL−1) after the addition of Mn(VII) (0.1 mM) and other different ions(Ln+)(10 mM). (e) The overlap between absorption spectrum of Mn(VII) (0.35 mM) and excitation and emission spectra of N,P-CDs. (f) Fluorescence decay of N,P-CDs (1 mg mL−1) in the absence and presence of Mn(VII) (200 μM) as a function of time at λex/λem of 386/478 nm.
Effect of on SMMC-7721 cells viability. (mean% ±SD, n = 6).

(a) LSCM images of SMMC-7721 cells incubated with 1 mg mL−1 N,P-CDs at 37℃for 2 h. The first, second and third panels are cell images taken at λex/λem of 405/422 ± 25, 488/500 ± 25 and 543/650 ± 25 nm, respectively. The fourth, fifth and sixth panels are the merged images of the first and second panels, second and third panels, and first and third panels, respectively. (b) LSCM images of SMMC-7721 cells incubated with 1 mg mL−1 N,P-CDs/Mn(VII) with different concentration (0, 0.05, 0.15 and 0.30 mM) at λex/λem of 405/450 ± 25 nm. The merged images with the bright field graphs, respectively. (c) The intensities of 20 cells were measured. Scale bar, 20 μm. Data are expressed as mean ± standard deviation.
Illustration of the synthetic procedure and fluorescence the assay for Mn(VII) based on the IFE of N,P-CDs.
Analytical results for the detection of Mn(VII) in Teas samples (n = 6, μg/g).
Analytical results for the detection of Mn(VII) in Chinese herbs samples (n = 6, μg/g).
The comparison of proposed method with the other analytical methods for Mn(VII) detection.

head.ce:title: Facile, rapid synthesis of N,P-dual-doped carbon dots as a label-free multifunctional nanosensor for Mn(VII) detection, temperature sensing and cellular imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Environmental Science and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan, 030006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Environmental Science and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Washington State University, Pullman, WA, 99164, USA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the synthetic procedure and fluorescence the assay for Mn(VII) based on the IFE of N,P-CDs.

We rapidly synthesized N,P-dual-doped carbon dots (N,P-CDs) via the acid-base neutralization spontaneous heat using a simple blend of phthalic acid, 1,2-ethylenediamine, and concentrated phosphoric acid. The approach is automatic without external heat treatment to speedily produce dozens of N,P-CDs, which refrain high temperature, sophisticated manipulations, and long reaction times. As-fabricated N,P-CDs with excellent luminescent properties, superior biocompatibility and respectable fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of 15% have been employed for the development of a label-free multifunctional fluorescence sensing platform for the detection of Mn(VII), temperature sensing and cellular imaging. Mn(VII) is able to effectively quench the fluorescence of N,P-CDs based on inner filter effect (IFE) because the absorption bands of Mn(VII) can perfectly overlap the emission and excitation wavelengths of N,P-CDs. The system had high selectivity toward Mn(VII) and the detection limit was 48.3 nM in the broad linear span from 10 μM to 200μM. Moreover, the N,P-CDs were extended for sensing Mn(VII) in living cell due to favorable biocompatibility and negligible cytotoxicity. Furthermore, the sensors showed a linear and reversible variation of emission intensity for a temperature range from 25℃ to 80℃, with a sensitivity of −1.79% per ℃. The practical implications of N,P-CDs for Mn(VII) detection have been demonstrated in Teas leaves and Medicinal herbs samples with satisfactory results.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Teas leaves and medicinal herbs samples

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) as a promising new class of carbon-based luminescent nanomaterials, have sparked a great deal of attention interdisciplinary scientists owing to their captivating properties such as their low toxicity, favorable biocompatibility, low cost and easy preparation [1,2]. These properties enable them to be promising candidates for numerous exciting applications such as bioimaging [3], drug delivery [4], sensors [5,6], and optoelectronic devices [7]. Since CDs were accidentally discovered during the purification of single-wall carbon nanotubes a decade ago [8]. Numerous synthetic methods have been developed for the preparation of CDs, including laser ablation [9,10], arc discharge [11], electrochemical oxidation [12], hydrothermal synthesis [13,14] and microwave assisted pyrolysis [15–17] etc. Among them, some involve harsh synthetic procedures, some require energy-consuming devices and even some are of cumbersome post-treatment. The facile synthesis of CDs remains to be explored. Our group presented the acid-base neutralization spontaneous heat method in which acid-base neutralization reaction provides a good automatic pathway without external heat treatment which takes advantage of spontaneous heat to carbonize carbon sources over other methods. For the improvement of luminescence of CDs concern, doping method with non-metallic heteroatomes is recognized to be a powerful approach to effectively tune their optical properties for sensing and bioimaging applications. N is a common dopant in the synthesis of CDs because not only its atomic size is comparable to carbon, but also it can bond strongly with the carbon atoms due to its five valence electrons, which provides potential active sites to enrich chemical activies of CDs. What’s more, CDs become n-type after doping with N atoms [18,19]. It has been demonstrated nitrogen containing organic molecules, such as ethylenediamine [20,21], long-chain polyethylene amines [22], and polyethylenimines [23], amino acids [24]can be used as N-dopants. For example, Wei et al. utilized the Maillard reaction between glucose and amino acids to obtain a series of N-doped CDs with high quantum yield [25]. Ding et al. reported the use of phthalic acid and 1,2-ethylenediamine to synthesize nitrogen-doped carbon for CQD-LEDs [26]. Through N-doping, CDs have shown to some extent fluorescence enhancement. However, main limitation of N-doped CDs is their fluorescence emission centered at shorter wavelength region. In comparison with N doping, phosphorus, in the same group with nitrogen, could favor the transition of electron with low energy, which provides the possibility to generate the long-wavelength emission [27]. In addition, phosphorus atom can form substitutional defects in diamond sp3 thin films, behaving as an n-type donor and thereby modifying the electronic and optical properties. Moreover, in view of the remarkable quantum-confinement and edge effects of carbon quantum dots, doping CDs with P atoms can alter their electronic characteristics and offer more active sites, thus yielding new and unanticipated properties [28]. Therefore, development of nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped carbon dots with distinctive characteristics is strongly desirable.

Manganese is a primary microelement for human beings and other species of organisms. There are different types of manganese oxidation state such as Mn(II) and Mn(VI). Mn(VII) is more hazardous to public health compared to other valence states, which is responsible for its greater stability and carcinogenic characteristics. The greatest exposure to manganese is usually from food stuffs, such as tea leafs. If the levels of Mn(VII) is abnormal, it can lead to some diseases [29–31]. For instance, excessive Mn(VII) cause DNA mutations and neurological disorders [32,33]. But deficiency of Mn(VII) can also result in physical retardation, abnormal function of bone and cartilage, poor reproductive performance and impaired glucose tolerance [34]. Accordingly, developing effective analytical methods for the sensitive and selective detection of Mn(VII) is highly desirable. Currently Mn(VII) detection methods comprise flame atomic absorption spectrophotometry [33,35], electrochemistry [36] and fluorescence spectrophotometry [37] etc. Nevertheless, the most of these methods usually suffer from relatively time-consuming, complicated sample preparation, sophisticated instrumentation and cost-ineffective in practice. Consequently, there is an urgent need to develop a convenient and rapid method to detect Mn(VII). Only rare sensors [37,38] are available for the determination of Mn(VII). Deng et al. developed a phosphorescence method rooted in L-cys-MnZnS QDs for the sensitive sensing of Mn(VII) by nonradiative interaction [39]. Ding et al. used a tyloxapol directly purchased as a sensing probe for detection of MnO4 − ions on the inner filter effect (IFE) [40]. However, the majority of probes are subjected to low quantum efficiency or the toxicity, which restrict practical application under biological conditions. Thus, the development of superior method with a simple, inexpensive, effective and selective becomes critical to enlarge the application.

Temperature as a basic physical parameter shows a significant influence on behavior of chemical and biological system. Due to its extensive applications, accurate temperature measurement is of fundamental importance [41]. To date, many instrumental techniques have been applied, including fluorescence spectrophotometry, scanning probe microscopy and raman spectroscopy [42–44]. Indeed, some fluorescent nanomaterials have been developed for temperature sensing, such as fluorescent polymers [45], semiconductor quantum dots [46] and organic dyes [47]. Inspired by the above nanomaterials, it may open up a new avenue for the construction of CDs-based temperature nanosensors.

Herein, we present a facile one-pot acid-base neutralization spontaneous synthesis method with low energy consumption, to prepare N,P-CDs using phthalic acid as the C source and 1,2-ethylenediamine, concentrated phosphoric acid as the N and P sources. As shown in Scheme 1 , 4.0 mL 1,2-ethylenediamine was mixed with 0.40 g phthalic acid and followed with the addition of 5.0 mL concentrated H3PO4 to produce bright blue fluorescent N,P-CDs. The as-synthesized fluorescent N,P-CDs were remarkably stable under extreme pH, ionic strengths, light illumination and long storage time while N,P-CDs have been utilised as IFE-based fluorescent nanosensors for the detection of Mn(VII) by an “on-off” fluorescence response. Strong fluorescence quenching effect of Mn(VII) on N,P-CDs based on inner filter effect (IFE) can be used for its selective detection among general metal ions because absorption spectrum of Mn(VII) has an overlap with the excitation and/or emission spectrum of the N,P-CDs. Compared with traditional FL nanoprobes, the proposed one seems to be more sensitive and selective, making them applicable for the detection of Mn(VII) in Teas leaves and Medicinal herbs samples with satisfactory results. What’s more, N,P-dual-doped carbon dots as a temperature probe based on the temperature-dependent response suffers from a FL intensity decrease within the range from 25℃ to 80℃. The change in FL intensity is appreciable, linear, and reversible for temperature changing. Most importantly, N,P-CDs were also successfully employed in intracellular imaging.

Phthalic acid, 1,2-ethylenediamine, Potassium permanganate (KMnO4) and Phosphoric acid were obtained from Shanghai Reagent (Shanghai, China). Other chemicals were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). All chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Four kinds of tea leaves samples including black tea (Yunnan, China), Longjing green tea (Hangzhou, China), Tieguanyin tea (Anxi, China), Pu'er tea (Yunnan, China) were purchased from local supermarket. Polygoni multiflora radix, Astragalus mongholicus, Ephedrae herba, Coptidis rhizome, Chinese wolfberry, Semen Ziziphi Spinosae and Forsythia were bought from a local pharmacy in Taiyuan, Shanxi, China. Doubly deionized water (DDI) was prepared on the basis of our published paper [4,6,48].

1,2-ethylenediamine (4.0 mL) and concentrated phosphoric acid (5.0 mL) are successively mixed with phthalic acid (0.4 g) in 100 mL beaker without stirring. By the acid-base neutralization spontaneous heat, the reaction mixture was quickly foamed and then gradually cooled to room temperature naturally within 5 min. The brownish-yellow product was dissolved in DDI and sonicated to obtain a transparent solution, and the supernatant was subjected to dialysis against water using a dialysis bag with MWCO of 1000 Da (Spectra/Por 6, Spectrum Laboratories, CA, USA) for further purification. Finally, the as-prepared pure N,P-CDs aqueous solutions were freeze-dried into powders.

The detection of Mn(VII) was carried out in 0.01 M PBS buffer (pH 7.4) at room temperature. In a typical assay, 100 u L N,P-CDs (20 mg mL−1) dispersion solution was diluted to 2 mL with PBS buffer (pH 7.4) in a quartz fluorescence cuvette. Then aliquots of a stock solution of Mn(VII) (5 mM) were added to N,P-CDs solutions and vibrated thoroughly, reaching a final concentration of 0.05–500 μM. To evaluate the influence of foreign substances, the FL intensities of N,P-CDs in the presence of various metal ions such as K+, Na+, Ag+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Co2+, Ba2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Al3+, Cr3+, and Mn(VII) and non-metal ions such as F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, SCN−, NO2 −, NO3 −, H2PO4 −, HPO4 2−, CO3 2−, S2O3 2−, SO4 2−, C2O4 2− were measured to study the selectivity of N,P-dual-doped carbon dots as fluorescence probes for sensing of Mn(VII). For the interference experiment, the above test ions solutions were mixed with N,P-CDs, and subsequently, Mn(VII) was added into the above mixture solution, and their FL intensities and spectra were measured respectively. The fluorescence measurements were acquired with excitation and emission slit width of 5 and 10 nm and a scan rate of 600 nm min−1.

The real samples including medicinal herb samples (Polygoni multiflori radix, Ephedrae herba, Astragalus mongholicus, Coptidis rhizome, Chinese wolfberry, Semen Ziziphi Spinosae and Forsythia) and teas (black tea, Longjing green tea leaves, Tieguanyin Tea and Pu'er tea), were dried in a vacuum oven at 80℃ for 2 h, cooled to room temperature and grounded into powders with a mortar. An accurately weighed sample of the medicine (2 mg) was transferred into a 100-mL volumetric flask. Subsequently, 5 mL of concentrated nitric acid and 2 mL of 30% (v/v) hydrogen peroxide were added and heated up to a complete digestion on a water bath. Afterward, the residue was dissolved and filtered through a 0.45 μm pore size filter membrane and then diluted with DDI to an appropriate concentration within the detection range.

The Human liver cancer SMMC-7721 cells in the exponential phase were seeded into 15 mm glass culture dishes at an initial density of 1 × 106 cells/mL with DMEM containing 10% FBS and incubated at 37℃ in a 5% CO2 incubator for 24 h. After incubation, the DMEM was discarded, and SMMC-7721 cells were treated with mixture of CDs (1 mg mL−1) and DMEM for 3 h. The extracellular CDs were removed by rinsing twice with phosphate buffer solutions (pH 7.4), and then cells were incubated with 1 mL PBS buffer (0.01 M) containing a range of concentration of Mn(VII). Finally, cellular fluorescence images were captured by a LSM880+Airyscan confocal laser scanning microscope (Carl Zeiss Co.Ltd.).

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images presented in Fig. 1 a showed as-formed N,P-CDs were nearly spherical and possessed a good monodispersity. After counting of the N,P-CDs of different sizes, the diameter distribution of N,P-dual-doped CDs was 2.00–5.60 nm with a mean particle diameter of 3.74 ± 0.40 nm (Fig. 1b). The DLS result indicated that the hydrate particle size of N,P-CD was approximately 11.63 nm (Fig. S1 ESI†), much larger than the size examined by TEM, which revealed the excellent hydrophilicity of N,P-CDs. Then, the elemental analysis, XPS and FT-IR were performed to characterize functionalization and chemical composition of N,P-dual-doped CDs. The elemental analysis results in Table S1A ESI† unveiled that the N,P-dual-doped carbon dots were made up of C 14.28, H 6.20, N 14.28, P 1.10 and O (calculated) 64.14 wt %, demonstrating that they are N,P-dual-doped CDs. The elemental contents were represented in terms of the relative number of atoms as depicted in Table S1B ESI†. The conventional formula for a N,P-CD was approximately C6H27N5P2O9. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the full-scan XPS of N,P-CDs exhibits distinct peaks locating at 132.2,189.8, 284.8, 399.9 and 528.7 eV, which are ascribed to P 2p, P 2 s, C1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s, respectively. In the high-resolution spectra, the C1 s band (Fig. 2b) can be mainly resolved into four peaks at 284.7, 286.0, 286.9, 288.5, which represent CC, CC/CN/CP, CO and OCO, respectively [49]. The N 1 s XPS signal (Fig. 2c) is disassembled into three surface constituents, which are contributed to N 1 s states in pyrrolic-N, NH and graphitic-N, corresponding to the binding energy of 401.1 eV, 402.1 eV and 403.8 eV, respectively [50,51]. The O1 s band can be fitted into three peaks (Fig. S2 ESI†), which are attributed to CO (530.8 eV), COH/COC (532.2 eV) and PO (533.3 eV) [49,52]. Deconvolution of the P 2p signal in Fig. 2d displays three components at 132.8, 133.6 and 135.4 eV, respectively, associating with C3 P, CP/C2 P, COP, respectively [49]. The XPS results further verified that N,P-CDs were functionalized by hydroxyl, carboxyl and simultaneously, N and P atoms were successfully doped onto the surface of the N,P-CDs. FT-IR spectroscopy was also employed to characterize N,P-CDs with the surface functional groups as shown in Fig. S3 ESI†. The broad characteristic band at 3613 cm−1 is related to the stretching vibration of OH/NH [53]. The diminutive peak at 2947 cm−1 is ascribed to the CH stretching [54]. Two sharp peaks located at about 1072 cm−1 and 958 cm−1 belong to the stretching vibration of POC and POH, respectively [55]. Other stretchings are CO (1695 cm−1), CO (1611 cm−1) and CN (1430 cm−1), along with the rocking vibrations of CH3 umbrella bend (1341 cm−1) [49,55,56]. The functional groups identified using the FTIR are in accordance with XPS analysis.

Fig. 3 exhibited the UV–vis absorption and corresponding fluorescence (FL) spectrum of synthesized N,P-CDs, confirming their remarkable optical properties. Fig. 3a, in the higher-energy UV region, the UV–vis absorption spectrum displayed a noticeable small shoulder at 273 nm, corresponding to the π–π* transitions of the aromatic sp2 in the N,P-CDs. The optimal excitation and emission wavelengths are fixed at 386 and 478 nm, respectively. The N,P-CDs aqueous solution are almost transparent under daylight and emit blue fluorescence under UV light (the inset of Fig. 3a). Like most luminescent CDs, the N,P-CDs showed the excitation wavelength dependent fluorescence behavior. The emission peaks shifted to longer wavelengths (from 280 nm to 460 nm) with increasing excitation wavelength (Fig. 3b). And the fluorescence intensity increased from 280 to 380 nm, achieved the maximum at 386 nm, but decreased by further extending the wavelength up to 460 nm. it is generally believed that such a property may be contributed to the difference in particle size (quantum effect) and different emissive trap sites on the surface of N,P-CDs [57,58].

Fluorescence stability performance is particularly significant for the CDs since this is a prerequisite for all practical applications. Herein, the effects of various concentrations of NaCl solution, pH, sustained UV irradiation exposure and storage time on the fluorescence intensity of CDs were investigated. As depicted in Fig. S4a ESI†, the fluorescence intensity of CDs is almost invariable in comparatively high ionic strength, indicating the CDs have great salt resistance. The fluorescence intensity of the CDs can not fluctuate a lot throughout the basic and acidic pH range (Fig. S4b ESI†), enabling it to work in a wide range of pH. N,P-CDs display stable fluorescent, which could be understood in terms of the change in surface charge owing to protonation-deprotonation. As shown in Fig. S4c ESI†, it is quite apparent that FL intensity of N,P-dual-doped CDs remains unvarying as the testing time increases with a 15 min increment. Even after a prolonged exposure of 90 min, the N,P-CDs still preserve 95% the initial intensity of N,P-dual-doped CDs. The re-dispersed N,P-CDs aqueous solutions are stable after three months storage in the solid state (Fig. S4d ESI†), demonstrating they possess favorable colloidal stability. Such remarkable photostability is attributed to the surface structure and composition of N,P-CDs. QY of the N,P-CDs was determined to be 15% using quinine sulfate as the reference (54% in 0.1 M H2SO4) (Fig. S5 ESI†), which is much higher than the non-doped CDs [59]. The increased quantum yield could be attributable to formation of p–n type photochemical diodes through nitrogen and phosphorus co-doping into carbon dots [56,60].

The CD undergoes a significant change in luminescence intensity upon heat treatment for a short period of time. It can be seen from Fig. 4 a that the FL intensity gradually decreases upon increasing temperature in temperature range from 25℃ to 80℃, it is ascribed to thermally activated population of non-radiative channels of surface (trap/defect) states [61,62]. At low temperature, the non-radiative channels are not thermally activated; therefore, the excited electrons can radiatively emit photons. Once the temperature is increased, the non-radiative channels become thermally activated; therefore, an increasing number of the excited electrons return to the ground state through a non-radiative process, and correspondingly the FL intensity decreases [61–64]. Therefore, it can be deduced that temperature increase caused aggregation of the CDs, which induced fluorescence quenching [65,66]. Concomitantly, the inset of Fig. 4a reveals that the response of the fluorescence intensity is linear in this range of temperatures with a temperature sensitivity of −1.79% per℃, obtaining the correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.997. To certify the reversible and recoverable fluorescence of the N,P-CDs, an experiment was carried out to observe the fluorescence variation during consecutive heating and cooling cycles over the temperature range 25–80℃. The reversible process can be readily repeated up to 8 cycles (Fig. 4b), and 83% of the maximum fluorescence intensity could still be retained. The result signified that the N,P-CDs exhibited marvelous reversibility and restorability whenever the temperature changed, which implied the temperature (25–80℃) cannot result in permanent destruction on the surface fluorescent structure of the as-prepared N,P-CDs. Fluorescent CDs are anticipated to apply as a promising temperature sensor, which makes it attractive for sensing applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimal conditions for fluorescent detection of Mn(VII)
ce:section.ce:para: The capability of N,P-CDs for the determination of Mn(VII) was investigated under the optimum experimental conditions. Specifically pH and reaction time between N,P-CDs and Mn(VII) were studied separately. Fig. S6a ESI† depicted the FL intensity of N,P-dual-doped carbon dots toward Mn(VII) in the pH range of 1–13. It is found that there is a negligible change of FL intensity of N,P-CDs in the pH range of 2–12. Taking into account the normal physiological pH values and the stability of Mn(VII), ultrapure water was selected for all the subsequent FL assays. Furthermore, the kinetic behavior of the N,P-CDs-Mn(VII) sensing system was surveyed and shown in Fig. S6b ESI†. It can be seen that the quenching process reached equilibrium in 40 s, indicating that response time was fairly rapid.

As shown in Fig. 5 a, upon the addition of Mn(VII) ranging from 0 to 500 M, significant fluorescence quenching can be observed. The inset of Fig. 5a further represents the relation between F0/F and different concentrations of Mn(VII) with an excellent linear behavior in the range from 10 to 200 μM. The Stern–Volmer equation was thus obtained as F0/F = 1.035 + KSV [Mn(VII)], where KSV is the SV quenching constant, F0 and F were the fluorescence intensities of CDs in the absence and presence of Mn(VII). The KSV is calculated to be 0.008 with a correlation coefficient R2 of 0.995. The limit of detection (LOD) was estimated to be 48.3 nM based on three times the standard deviation of blank divided by the slope of the calibration graph. Our results imply that the proposed fluorescence nanosensor based on N,P-CDs is propitious to label-free determination of the concentration of Mn(VII). In order to understand effect of environmental temperature on Mn(VII) detection, temperature dependent FL behavior of the Mn(VII)/N,P-CDs system were investigated at the temperature of 10℃, 25℃ and 50℃ and displayed in Fig. 5(b). The calculated values of Ksv are 0.0102 (R2 = 0.999), 0.0085 (R2 = 0.99938) and 0.00608 (R2 = 0.99924) for 10, 25 and 50 °C, respectively. The Ksv values decrease with increasing temperature. The above results illustrate that Mn(VII) detection based on N,P-CDs appears to be more sensitive at temperatures of 10 °C than at temperature of 25℃ and 50℃.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity of the fluorescent probe for Mn (VII)
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the selectivity of N,P-CDs to Mn (VII), fluorescence influences of thirty-one sorts of common metal ions (K+, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Ag+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Ba2+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Al3+, Fe2+,Cr3+ and Mn (VII)) and anion ions (F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, SCN−, NO2 −,NO3 −, H2PO4 −, HPO4 2−, CO3 2−, S2O3 2−, SO4 2−, C2O4 2−) on CDs were tested. As shown in Fig. 5(c), among them, Mn (VII) afford the most significant fluorescence quenching effect. And no obvious intensity change was observed in the case of the other ions, indicating highly selective sensing of Mn(VII) for N,P-CDs. As reported in the some literature, Fe3+ can dramatically quench the fluorescence peak of N,P-CDs. It is attributed to the formation of stable metal complexes because of strong chemical bonding between N,P-CDs and Fe3+, and electron or charge transfer [19,67,68]. In this work, N,P-CDs have excellent selectivity for Mn (VII) assay based on inner filter effect (IFE). The excitation and emission of N,P-CDs have a perfect overlap with the absorption spectrum of Mn(VII) (vide infra), and there is no overlap in the absorption spectrum of Fe3+ under the same test conditions (Fig. S7 ESI†). Therefore, Fe3+ is a negligible effect on the Mn(VII) detection. In addition, the selectivity of this sensing system in the presence of possible interference ions was evaluated considering the cross reactivity. It was found that, apart from Mn(VII) at a concentration of 0.1 mM, the most of metal ions and anion ions at ultrahigh concentrations (10 mM) do not induce significant quenching on FL intensity of N,P-CDs, demonstrating that the approved method for determination of Mn(VII) has good antijamming capability Fig. 5(d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of Mn(VII) assay based on IFE
Application of Mn(VII) detection in real tea samples and medicinal herb samples
ce:section.ce:para:
The feasibility of this nanoprobe based on label-free CDs for the detection of Mn(VII) in different tea samples and Chinese herbs samples was validated by the standard addition method. The results and the recovery for the spiked samples are listed in Tables 1 and 2
. It can be seen that the Mn(VII) contents in Teas samples such black tea, Longjing green tea, Tieguanyin tea, Pu'er tea detected by the fabricated N,P-CDs probe were 93.5, 29.9, 83.5 and 61.1 μg/g, respectively. The recoveries range from 93.1% to 108.1% after adding a given amount of Mn(VII) in standard solutions, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) of six replicate measurements are between 4.14% and 8.51%. Besides, application of the N,P-CDs nanoprobe for Mn(VII) sensing in various kinds of medicinal herb samples was investigated further. As shown in Table 2, the amounts of Mn(VII) in medicinal herb samples, such as Astragalus mongholicus, Coptidis rhizome, Semen Ziziphi Spinosae, Polygoni multiflori radix, Chinese wolfberry, Forsythia and Ephedrae herba, detected by the fabricated “on-off” sensor, were 23.6, 73.0, 21.1, 41.6, 12.2, 33.9 and 62.5 μg/g, respectively. It is worth noting that quantitative spike recoveries for the determination of Mn(VII) in these above real samples ranged from 95.6% to 107.6%, with a relative standard deviation of less than 9.51% (n = 6). These observations further confirmed that constructed a label-free N,P-CDs probe for monitoring Mn(VII) in Teas samples and Chinese herbs samples were reliable and feasible. The comparison of proposed method with the other analytical methods for Mn(VII) detection is listed in Table 3 . Our proposed method are comparable or even better in sensitivity than those reported [39,40,69–71]. Although the SEP-FAAS [72] possesses higher sensitivity than ours, it requires large-scale instrument and large secondary wastes, restricting the practical on-site detection. The proposed method herein is simple and cost effective, and sufficient to monitor Mn (VII) at the extreme low level.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cellular imaging determination of Mn(VII)

ce:section.ce:para: As stated previously, phthalic acid, 1,2-ethylenediamine and concentrated phosphoric acid were employed as precursors facilitating the formation of remarkably stable N,P-CDs with a respectable QY of 15%. The as-fabricated N,P-CDs displayed high chemical and highly durable fluorescence stabilities. Through chemical composition and structural analysis, the N,P-CDs are confirmed to contain functional groups, which lead to highly sensitive and selective response to Mn(VII). These properties make N,P-CDs appropriate to work as a nanoprobe for determination of Mn(VII). It is presumed that the Mn (VII)-mediated FL N,P-CDs quenching is attributed to IFE. Explorations of Mn(VII) detection in Teas leaves and Medicinal herbs samples manifested the ability of this sensor to work effectively. Notably, the temperature effect on the FL intensity made the N,P-CDs potential as a temperature probe with superior linearity, sensitivity and reversibility. Most importantly, the cellular uptake demonstrated that the constructed N,P-CDs behaved as an underlying candidate for fluorescent cellular imaging.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21705101, 21475080 and 21575084) and Shanxi Province Hundred Talents Project. We also acknowledge Dr. Juanjuan Wang from the Scientific Instrument Center at Shanxi University for her help with Zeiss LSM880 confocal laser-scanning microscope measurements.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.09.027.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Fangfang Du received her BSc degree in Chemistry from Lvliang University in 2015. She is currently pursuing his PhD degree at Shanxi University in 2018 and major in environmental science. She has published research articles in Talanta. Her current research interests include nanomaterial and chemosensors.
Gan Li obtained B.Sc. degree in Pharmaceutical engineering from Shanxi Agricultural University in 2016. Now she is a postgraduate in Shanxi University and major in medicine analysis.

Xiaojuan Gong received her BSc degree and PhD degree in environmental science in 2011 and 2016 from Shanxi University, respectively. She is currently a professor in Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University. She has published more than twenty research articles extensively in journals such as ACS Applied Materials & Interfaces, Nanoscale, Journal of Materials Chemistry B, Analytica Chimica Acta, Talanta, etc. Her research interests are in the areas of carbon nanoparticles, chemical and biological sensor, environmental analysis, and diagnosis and therapy of cancer.
Zhonghui Guo received BSc degree in Pharmaceutical engineering from Mudanjiang Medical University in 2017. Now she is a postgraduate in Shanxi University and major in medicine analysis.

Shaomin Shuang is a professor of Analytical Chemistry at Shanxi University. She received her Ph.D. degree in South China University of Technology in 1998. She has published extensively in journals such as Nanoscale,Journal of Materials Chemistry B, Chemical Communications, Sensors and Actuators B, Analytical Chemistry, Talanta etc. Her research interests are in the areas of analytical chemistry, supermolecular chemsitry, and photochemsity.

Ming Xian is currently the Ralph G. Yount Distinguished Professor and Associate Chair in the Chemistry Department at the Washington State University, Pullman. She has published more than hundred research articles extensively in journals such as Angew. Chem. Int. Ed., J. Am. Chem. Soc., Chem. Soc. Rev, Org Lett., Chemical Science, Chem. Commun, etc. His research focuses on the development of novel chemistry and chemical tools to facilitate biological studies of reactive sulfur species.

Chuan Dong is a professor of Analytical Chemistry at Shanxi University. He obtained his Ph.D. degree at Shanxi University in 2002. Dong has published over 200 papers and 100 patents and his current research interests are biosensors, chemosensors, optical fiber sensor, fluorescence and room temperature phosphorescence, and environmental analysis and monitoring.
